header.subtitle: Module 8: Agents, Sub-Agents & Agent Teams
sec.single.intro: The starting point. A single Claude with a single context window processes everything sequentially. For most day-to-day tasks, this is exactly what you need, the cheapest and most predictable way to work.
sec.single.ideal.title: Ideal for
sec.single.ideal.li.1: Tasks with a clear, well-defined scope
sec.single.ideal.li.2: Files that fit within the context window
sec.single.ideal.li.3: Sequential work where each step depends on the previous one
sec.single.wall.title: Where it hits the wall
sec.single.wall.li.1: Large repositories with hundreds of relevant files
sec.single.wall.li.2: Independent tasks that could run in parallel
sec.single.wall.li.3: Context fatigue: from ~60–70% of the window, quality drops
sec.single.wall.li.4: Research that would require exploring many paths simultaneously
sec.single.fatigue.title: Context Degradation (Context Fatigue)
sec.single.fatigue.desc: From ~70% of the context window, agents start forgetting earlier instructions, repeating work, and producing less accurate responses. Recognizing this limit is the first step to knowing when to scale up.
sec.single.tip: Rule of thumb: If you can describe the task in one paragraph and it doesn't require exploring more than ~30 files, a single agent is enough. When the task starts demanding parallelism or the context gets crowded, it's time to scale up.
sec.porque.intro: Multi-agent systems work mainly because they allow spending enough tokens to solve the problem. Anthropic's research is clear: token usage explains 80% of the performance variance. More agents = more parallel processing = better results.
sec.porque.chart.label: Data from research · Anthropic Multi-Agent Research System
sec.porque.stat.1.desc: performance improvement with multi-agent vs single agent on research tasks
sec.porque.stat.2.desc: of performance variance explained by token volume used
sec.porque.stat.3.desc: time reduction with parallel tool calling instead of sequential
sec.porque.analogy.label: Why this works: the Anthropic analogy
sec.porque.analogy.quote: "Humans individually haven't gotten much smarter in the last 100,000 years, but societies have become exponentially more capable through collective intelligence and coordination. Multi-agents do the same with AI."
sec.porque.case.1.title: Parallelism
sec.porque.case.1.desc: Tasks that can run simultaneously: 5 agents review 5 modules at the same time instead of sequentially.
sec.porque.case.2.title: Scale
sec.porque.case.2.desc: Large repositories that don't fit in a single context. Each agent explores an area without polluting the others.
sec.porque.case.3.title: Specialization
sec.porque.case.3.desc: A security reviewer agent with context focused on OWASP produces better results than an overloaded generalist.
sec.subagentes.intro: Each sub-agent has isolated context and reports back to the orchestrator. But what most descriptions oversimplify: sub-agents don't need to run in parallel. You can, and often should, orchestrate a sequential pipeline with dependencies, where the output of one agent becomes the input of the next. The entire architecture is defined by you in the orchestrator agent's instructions.
sec.subagentes.parallel.desc: The orchestrator spawns multiple sub-agents at the same time for independent tasks. Each one works in isolation and reports to the parent. Good for exploration, research, and reviews that don't depend on each other.
sec.subagentes.pipeline.desc: The orchestrator defines a chain: each agent receives the output of the previous one and passes it to the next. Allows feedback loops (e.g., Programmer <-> Reviewer until pass). The human writes all this logic in the orchestrator's instruction file.
sec.subagentes.pipeline.human.desc: Human: delivers the task and approves the plan before execution
sec.subagentes.pipeline.po.desc: Project Owner: synthesizes the task and passes it to the PM
sec.subagentes.pipeline.pm.desc: Project Manager: orchestrates the entire sequence, distributes tasks and receives reports
sec.subagentes.pipeline.pm.arrow: research task (can split into N researchers)
sec.subagentes.pipeline.researcher.desc: Researcher (Read Only): explores code, docs, and dependencies. PM can spawn multiple in parallel if needed
sec.subagentes.pipeline.secretary1.desc: Secretary: debates the plan with PM, Researcher, and PO. If there are unanswered questions, the PO asks the human. When consensus is reached, writes plan.md for human approval
sec.subagentes.pipeline.secretary1.arrow: human approves plan.md → PM delegates to Programmer
sec.subagentes.pipeline.programmer.desc: Programmer: implements exactly what's in the approved plan, reports to PM
sec.subagentes.pipeline.reviewer.desc: Reviewer: reviews the code and reports to PM. If there are bugs, PM sends it back to the Programmer. Loop until "pass".
sec.subagentes.pipeline.security.desc: Security Review: security audit. Same loop: failures go back to Programmer via PM until approved.
sec.subagentes.pipeline.secretary2.desc: Secretary: updates CHANGELOG.md, MEMORY.md, and final documentation
sec.subagentes.pipeline.note: The human defines this entire architecture in the orchestrator agent's instructions (CLAUDE.md or the PM file). Claude doesn't invent the hierarchy. It executes the workflow you specified.
sec.subagentes.agents.title: Defining each agent in .claude/agents/
sec.subagentes.agents.desc: Each pipeline role becomes an .md file with frontmatter, allowed tools, and task instructions.
sec.subagentes.builtin.title: Built-in agent types (agent: field)
sec.subagentes.builtin.gp.desc: Full toolset. Default when no type is specified.
sec.subagentes.builtin.explore.desc: Read-only tools optimized for exploring codebases. Cannot edit files.
sec.subagentes.builtin.plan.desc: Focused on architecture and planning. No direct code execution.
sec.teams.intro: Agent Teams goes beyond sub-agents: team members communicate directly with each other via a shared mailbox system. A team lead coordinates, but teammates aren't subordinates, they're peers who collaborate.
sec.teams.flow.title: Communication flow: peer-to-peer network
sec.teams.flow.note: Teammates communicate directly, not just with the lead
sec.teams.config.title: Configuration
sec.teams.usecase.1.title: Parallel code review
sec.teams.usecase.1.desc: 3 teammates review simultaneously: security, performance, and test coverage. Each with context focused on their domain.
sec.teams.usecase.2.title: Competing hypotheses
sec.teams.usecase.2.desc: 5 teammates investigate 5 different theories for a complex bug simultaneously and debate results via mailbox.
sec.teams.usecase.3.title: Cross-layer coordination
sec.teams.usecase.3.desc: Frontend, backend, and DB work in parallel on a feature, coordinating interfaces via shared task list.
sec.teams.limitations.title: Important limitations
sec.teams.limitations.li.2: Only 1 team per session, no nested teams
sec.teams.limitations.li.3: Risk of conflict on files edited by 2 teammates
sec.comparacao.intro: The right choice depends on three variables: task complexity, whether the parts are independent, and whether you need coordination between agents.
sec.comparacao.row.comm.label: Communication
sec.comparacao.row.coord.label: Coordination
sec.comparacao.row.ctx.label: Context
sec.comparacao.row.cost.label: Token cost
sec.comparacao.row.stability.label: Stability
sec.comparacao.row.best.label: Best for
sec.comparacao.tree.title: Decision tree
sec.comparacao.tree.q1: Does the task fit ~30 files and is it sequential? → Single Agent
sec.comparacao.tree.q2: Are the sub-tasks independent with no need for coordination between them? → Sub-Agents
sec.comparacao.tree.q3: Do agents need to coordinate results with each other during execution? → Agent Teams (experimental)
sec.comparacao.tree.q4: Do the sub-tasks have high interdependency or need shared context? → Don't use multi-agent. The overhead will outweigh the gains.
sec.tokens.intro: Multi-agents are powerful but expensive. Agent Teams use ~15× more tokens than a standard chat. The question isn't "can I use multi-agent?", it's "does the task justify this cost?"
sec.tokens.card.1.title: Standard chat
sec.tokens.card.2.title: Sub-Agents
sec.tokens.card.3.title: Agent Teams
sec.tokens.patterns.title: Patterns that maximize value per token
sec.tokens.pattern.1.label: Pattern
sec.tokens.pattern.1.title: Plan Then Build · Rocklin
sec.tokens.pattern.1.desc: A planner agent creates a detailed blueprint. A fresh agent receives the plan and executes, without the planner's polluted history and context. The executor starts clean, focused on delivery.
sec.tokens.pattern.2.label: Pattern
sec.tokens.pattern.2.title: Ralph Loop · Osmani
sec.tokens.pattern.2.desc: Atomic 5-step workflow to avoid context overflow while keeping history in git:
sec.tokens.pattern.2.note: The context reset in step 5 prevents context fatigue: the agent starts the next task with a clean window.
sec.tokens.pattern.3.label: Pattern
sec.tokens.pattern.3.title: Independent Review · Rocklin
sec.tokens.pattern.3.desc: "Having an agent review its own work is outdated." A second agent, without context from the generation process, finds more bugs. The same creation bias that affects humans affects AIs. Always separate generator from reviewer.
sec.limites.intro: Multi-agents amplify everything, including errors. A small spec error, which with a single agent would be a local problem, multiplies across all agents running in parallel.
sec.limites.anti.1.title: Vague spec with multiple agents
sec.limites.anti.1.desc: "When you orchestrate 50 agents in parallel, fuzzy thinking doesn't slow you down, it multiplies you." - Osmani. Each agent will interpret the ambiguity differently. The result is chaotic and costly to fix.
sec.limites.anti.2.title: Precise spec before scaling
sec.limites.anti.2.desc: Invest time in specifying clearly: scope, expected output, dependencies, what NOT to do. Spec quality is leverage: the same effort that produces a mediocre spec for 1 agent produces an excellent spec for 10.
sec.limites.anti.3.title: Two agents editing the same file
sec.limites.anti.3.desc: In Agent Teams, if two teammates edit the same file simultaneously, the second overwrites the first. There's no automatic merge. Plan file ownership before starting the team.
sec.limites.anti.4.title: Clear file ownership per agent
sec.limites.anti.4.desc: Assign in the spec: "Teammate A owns src/auth/. Teammate B owns src/api/." Isolated git worktrees per agent eliminate this risk.
sec.limites.anti.5.title: Delegating architectural judgment
sec.limites.anti.5.desc: Decisions like "which architecture to use", "what not to build", and "which tradeoffs to accept" should not be delegated. Agents execute, but you judge.
sec.limites.anti.6.title: Delegate tasks, not judgment
sec.limites.anti.6.desc: Keep architecture, scope, and prioritization decisions with humans. Delegate implementation, boilerplate, migrations, and tasks with well-defined criteria.
sec.limites.warning.title: Parallel isn't always faster · Rocklin
sec.limites.warning.desc: In a real-world software development case study, sequential execution with less parallelism produced better results than parallel execution. Coordination overhead frequently outweighs the speed gain. Measure before assuming that more agents = faster.
sec.proximos.intro: Start with the simplest pattern. Scale up only when the problem demands it.
sec.proximos.step.1.title: Identify a task you already do sequentially that could be parallelized
sec.proximos.step.1.desc: Classic example: PR review. Instead of asking "review my code", use 3 sub-agents focused on security, performance, and tests. Compare the quality of the result.
sec.proximos.step.2.title: Create your first custom agent in .claude/agents/
sec.proximos.step.2.desc: Start with a review agent focused on your project's domain. Define the role, tools, and output format.
sec.proximos.step.3.title: Try the "Plan Then Build" pattern on a real task
sec.proximos.step.3.desc: Use one agent to create the plan, paste the result into a fresh second agent to execute. Observe the difference in precision and quality versus doing everything in one session.
sec.proximos.step.4.title: Agent Teams: enable experimental mode and test with parallel review
sec.proximos.step.4.desc: When you're comfortable with sub-agents, try Agent Teams on a real PR. Define clear file ownership before starting.
sec.proximos.mantra.label: Part 8 Mantra:
sec.proximos.mantra.text: "In multi-agent systems, your most valuable contribution isn't writing code, it's specifying the problem with enough clarity that multiple agents can solve independent parts without getting in each other's way."
